currentUse: Catholic church building (mission of the Cathedral parish, Archdiocese of Hartford) at 53 Capitol Ave. Regular Masses — including the Sunday 9:00 am Lithuanian/English Mass — were announced to end May 30, 2026; funerals and baptisms continue. Parishioners are campaigning to convert the building into a Lithuanian cultural center.
summary: Holy Trinity (Švč. Trejybės) Lithuanian Roman Catholic Church still stands at 53 Capitol Avenue in Hartford, but the parish has been through a devastating eighteen months. In late January 2025 the Archdiocese of Hartford placed its longtime priest, Fr. Charles Jacobs, on administrative leave over alleged financial improprieties, stating he had admitted taking an undisclosed amount of parish money for personal use; Very Rev. John Melnick, rector of the Cathedral, was appointed administrator, and the February 2025 bulletin confirmed the parish is canonically a mission of the Cathedral parish. In August 2025 Hartford police closed their larceny investigation for insufficient evidence, but the archdiocese kept Jacobs on leave pending a canonical process and did not return him to the parish. In April 2026 parishioners were notified of a partial closure: citing a loss of over $74,000 since March 2025, a lack of clergy, and unaffordable repairs, leadership announced that Masses would cease as of May 30, 2026, while funerals and baptisms would continue. As of the February 2025 bulletin the parish (about 300 families drawing from 47 Connecticut zip codes) still celebrated a Sunday 9:00 am Lithuanian/English Mass. Parishioners now hope to turn the historic 1928 church into a cultural center celebrating Hartford's Lithuanian roots.

developments.headline: Longtime priest Fr. Charles Jacobs placed on administrative leave over alleged financial improprieties
developments.detail: The Archdiocese of Hartford announced to parishioners that Fr. Charles Jacobs had been placed on administrative leave amid an investigation into parish finances; the archdiocese statement (printed in the Feb 9, 2025 parish bulletin) said Jacobs 'has taken an undisclosed amount of money from the parish for his unauthorized and personal uses,' a characterization later disputed by Jacobs and supporters. Very Rev. John Melnick, rector of the Cathedral, was appointed administrator, with Rev. Francis Snell assisting; the bulletin confirmed Holy Trinity is canonically a mission of the Cathedral parish and assured parishioners the church was financially stable and weekend Masses (including the 9:00 am Lithuanian/English Sunday Mass) would continue. WFSB reported the parish counts roughly 300 families from 47 Connecticut zip codes.

sources.title: Holy Trinity Roman Catholic Church bulletin, 5th Sunday in Ordinary Time (archdiocese statement to parishioners)
Popular CT priest under investigation for allegedly stealing money from parish

developments.headline: Hartford police close larceny investigation without charges; archdiocese keeps Jacobs on leave pending canonical process
developments.detail: Hartford Police determined there was insufficient evidence to charge Fr. Jacobs in the larceny investigation opened in January 2025. The Archdiocese of Hartford said Jacobs nonetheless still faces an internal canonical process; he retained a canon lawyer, remains on administrative leave with salary and benefits, and was not returned to Holy Trinity.

sources.title: Priest won't face charges after larceny at Hartford church: police
Hartford police end investigation into priest accused of stealing money

developments.headline: Partial closure announced: Masses to end May 30, 2026; parishioners push for a Lithuanian cultural center
developments.detail: Parishioners were told after Mass that Holy Trinity would partially close due to financial woes and a lack of clergy: regular Masses end as of May 30, while funerals and baptisms continue. Leadership reported a loss of over $74,000 since March 2025 despite reduced expenses, and a parishioner cited repairs the parish could not afford. Parishioners including Susan Gudaitis expressed hope of turning the 100+-year-old church into a cultural center celebrating Hartford's Lithuanian roots; attendee Dalia Giedrimiene said 'Where will we go? I don't have any other place to go.'

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions, but a major refinement: the archive record's "standing / active as of late 2023" is now outdated. The building still stands and the parish survived into 2026, but regular Masses (including the Lithuanian Sunday Mass, still listed in the Feb 2025 bulletin) were announced to end May 30, 2026 in a "partial closure" driven by a $74,000+ loss since March 2025, clergy shortage, and the January 2025 Fr. Charles Jacobs financial scandal. Also a refinement of ownership context: the Feb 2025 bulletin confirms Holy Trinity is canonically a mission of the Cathedral (St. Joseph) parish, not a standalone parish — consistent with "diocese_rc" ownership but more precise. Nothing found that speaks to the archive's 2017 hall-only-access ambiguity.
gaps: Could not verify: (1) the exact publication date of the WTNH partial-closure article (site returned 403; AMP version gave only "3 months ago" from July 2026, i.e. ~April 2026); (2) whether Masses in fact ceased after May 30, 2026, and whether a final/farewell Mass was held — no post-May-2026 coverage found; (3) the outcome of the canonical process against Fr. Jacobs (still pending as of Aug 2025 reporting); (4) any formal organization, fundraising, or agreement behind the proposed Lithuanian cultural center — only parishioner hopes reported, and a "Holy Trinity Cultural Arts Center" Facebook page surfaced in search but could not be opened to confirm it relates to Hartford; (5) when Holy Trinity became a mission of the Cathedral parish (possibly the 2017 archdiocesan restructuring, but no source directly ties the two); (6) the parish website (holytrinityhartford.org) blocked fetching (HTTP 403), so its current announcements could not be read — note its odd spam-looking subdomains in search results suggest possible site compromise; (7) no Draugas or other Lithuanian-language coverage of the 2026 closure was found via search.
